title: Regelia coriacea
intro: Regelia coriacea, commonly known as Black Pea Bush, is a shrub native to Western Australia. This plant has been traditionally used by Indigenous Australians to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and sore throats, due to its antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Regelia coriacea include phenolic acids, flavonoids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, Regelia coriacea is often used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are commonly prepared as remedies for colds, coughs, and respiratory issues.
taxonomy: Regelia coriacea belongs to the family Myoporaceae, which is a small group of flowering plants native to Australia. The genus Regelia is a group of shrubs that are commonly found in Western Australia. Regelia coriacea is a species within this genus and is known to have a distinctive appearance. The classification of Regelia coriacea is further divided into different species and subspecies, each with unique characteristics. This classification helps in understanding the diversity and distribution of the Regelia coriacea plant.
